title: Low Surface Energy Coatings from Waterborne Nano-Dispersions of Polymer Complexes
abstract:
[4] J. Kopp, H. Bannasch, C. Andree, G. B. Stark, Langenbecks Arch.
Chir. 1996, S1, 229.
[5] K. Kubo, N. Tsukasa, M. Uehara, Y. Izumi, M. Ogino, M. Kitano, T.
Sueda, J. Oral Rehabil. 1997, 24, 70.
[6] L. T. Canham, Adv. Mater. 1995, 7, 1033.
[7] S. C. Bayliss, L. Buckberry, P. J. Harris, C. Rousseau, Thin Solid Films
1997, 297, 308.
[8] S. C. Bayliss, P. J. Harris, L. Buckberry, C. Rousseau, J. Mater. Sci. Lett.
1997, 16, 737.
[9] A. Curtis, C. Wilkinson, Biomaterials 1998, 18, 1573.
[10] S. C. Bayliss, P. J. Harris, L. D. Buckberry, J. Porous Mater., in press.
[11] L. D. Buckberry, H. J. Adcock, J. Adler, I. S. Blagbrough, P. J. Gaskin,
P. N. Shaw, ATLA, Altern. Lab. Anim. 1994, 22, 72.
Low Surface Energy Coatings from Waterborne
Nano-Dispersions of Polymer Complexes
By Andreas F. Thünemann,* Antje Lieske, and Bernd-
Reiner Paulke
Fluorocarbon materials are unique in their ability to
repel both oil and water. There has been great develop-
mental work optimizing fluorocarbon polymers as ultra
low-adhesive materials since the discovery of poly(tetra-
fluoroethylene)[1] over fifty years ago. Commercially avail-
able fluorinated side-chain acrylic and methacrylic poly-
mers are typical low surface energy coating materials.[2]
Amongst the numerous molecular structures investigated,
a close-packed uniform CF3 surface was found to possess
the lowest surface tension ever measured (6 mN/m).[3] A
polymeric material that can maintain a stable, uniform CF3
surface may play a key role in producing non-adhesive ma-
terials with surface energies much lower than that of poly-
(tetrafluoroethylene) (20 mN/m). Applications of such a
material are manifold, ranging from protection from graf-
fiti[4] to coatings with anti-fouling properties.[5] Promising
strategies for realizing surfaces that are highly enriched
with CF3 groups include the development of self-organizing
materials such as Langmuir-Blodgett monolayers,[6,7] fluori-
nated block copolymers,[8,9] semifluorinated side-chain
ionenes,[10] fluorinated poly(a,L-glutamate)s,[11] and solid
complexes of polyelectrolytes with fluorinated surfac-
tants.[12,13] Of these promising new low surface energy
materials, the one that successfully combines low cost and
ecologically friendly synthesis has the best chance of being
introduced for daily use. A key factor here is avoidance of
organic solvents. In earlier articles we showed that fluori-
nated polyelectrolyte surfactant complexes could be pre-
pared from aqueous solution.[12,13] However, to date, coat-
ings of these materials are prepared from organic solutions.
In this paper we report a waterborne nano-dispersion of a
new complex which can be applied to smooth surfaces, re-
sulting in thin films with surface energies significantly be-
low that of poly(tetrafluoroethylene). Imperatively, the op-
tical appearance of treated surfaces is not affected by such
a coating.
For complexation we used two commercially available
compounds: Poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI), a branched, water-
soluble polymer that is widely used in the paper industry.[14]
At low pH, PEI has the highest known charge density of all
polyelectrolytes. The second compound, Fluowet SB, is a
surfactant made from sulfosuccinic acid, which is esterified
with fluorinated side chains. Fluowet SB is regularly used
as a spreading agent.[15] The complexation of PEI with
Fluowet SB (stoichiometric complex with respect to
charge) was performed in water by adding solutions of PEI
to a dilute micellar solution of Fluowet SB. In contrast to
the procedure described earlier,[12,13] macroscopic precipi-
tation of the complex was avoided by using an excess of
Fluowet SB to aid dispersion. The diameters of the dis-
persed complex particles were about 200 nm, as deter-
mined by dynamic light scattering. A dilute (0.3 % w/w)
dispersion of PEI-Fluowet SB was deposited on smooth,
but chemically different, surfaces such as glass, aluminum,
car, and airplane sheets. The coated surfaces were dried,
washed extensively with water, and finally polished. By this
procedure, transparent, thin coatings of about 400 nm
thickness were achieved that were invisible to the human
eye. These coatings exhibited highly enhanced contact an-
gles of the test liquids deposited on each surface, indicating
a significant reduction of the surface energies (Table 1).
The surface free energy of a solid can be used as a guide
to its relative adhesive properties. However, it is not a
straightforward exercise to measure the surface free energy
of solids directly. For practical reasons, procedures based
on contact angle measurements are regularly used.[10,16±20]
Dynamic contact angles (y) of test liquids with different
surface tensions were measured by means of the sessile
drop method (Table 1).[21] It is found, in particular, that all
non-polar liquids that spread readily on the pristine sur-
faces show high contact angles on complex-coated surfaces.
From the contact angles of a homologous series of non-
polar liquids the critical surface tension gc of a surface can
be determined as introduced by Zisman.[16] gc defines the
wettability of a solid by denoting the lowest surface tension
a liquid can have and still exhibit a contact angle greater
than zero degrees. The gc of a surface is usually obtained
from a Zisman plot of contact angles;[16] i.e., a plot of cos y
versus the surface tensions of non-polar liquids gl, with the
extrapolation of gl to cos y = 1 giving gc. Zisman plots result
in critical surface tensions varying from 13.1 mN/m to
5.7 mN/m depending on the coated material (Fig. 1 and
Table 2). The latter value is identical to the lowest critical
surface tension encountered to date,[22] which was obtained
for an oriented close-packed monolayer of perfluorododec-
anoic acid. It is widely accepted that such low critical
surface tensions characterize the resulting properties of a
surface of closed-packed perfluoromethyl CF3 groups.[8,23]
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 4

Fraunhofer Institut für Angewandte Polymerforschung
Kantstrasse 55, D-14513 Teltow (Germany)

322

0935-9648/99/0403-0322 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 4
Therefore, we conclude that the complex-coated surfaces
presented here must be strongly enriched with CF3 groups.
It must be stressed that although gc values are widely used
for surface characterization, there is no theoretical justi-
fication for equating the critical surface tension with
the surface free energy of a material. The wettability data
were
also
used
to
determine
the
dispersion
force
component of the surface energy gd
s by using the Girifalco±
Good±Fowkes±Young equation,[17,18] which has the form
cos y = ±1 + 2(gd
s / gl)1/2 and was theoretically justified. This
equation is utilized by plotting cos y versus (1/gl)1/2 for a
family of dispersive liquids with the ordinate of the best lin-
ear fit being ±1 and the gradient giving 2(gd
s)1/2. Figure 2
shows such Girifalco±Good±Fowkes±Young plots for the
dynamic advancing contact angle data obtained for glass
and aluminium.
Linear fits result in dispersive surface energies of 10.2 to
10.7 mN/m for the complex coating, independent of the sub-
strate (Table 2). These values are close to those found for
the highly fluorinated side-chain homopolymer poly[(per-
fluorooctylethylene)oxymethyl]styrene
(9.3 mN/m[20]).
Both gc and gd
s values indicate a rather ordered packing of
the perfluorinated segments, thus exposing CF3 groups at
the surface. It is noteworthy that the contact angles of water
(the most polar test liquid, gl = 72.7 mN/m) are smaller than
those of methylene iodide (gl = 50.0 mN/m) and that these
depend strongly on the coated surface (y is higher for a car
sheet than for a glass slide). This provides clear evidence of
surface reconstruction of the complex surface when in con-
tact with water. Reconstruction phenomena are widespread
in polymer coatings and have been observed for self-orga-
nizing semifluorinated side-chain ionenes,[10] and even poly-
mers containing only a few polar groups such as Teflon-
PFA.[23]
Table 1. List of contact angles of different solvents on materials coated with
complex dispersion at 20 C. Listed are the surface tensions of the test liq-
uids, advancing, stationary and receding contact angles measured by a Krüss
contact angle goniometer.
[a] Error of measurement ca. ±1. [b] Commercial product of poly-(di-
methylsiloxane) oligomers (trimethylsiloxy terminated) from Geltest Inc.
Fig. 1. Zisman plots for different poly(ethyleneimine)-Fluowet SB coated
surfaces. Points are obtained with (1) hexane, gl = 18.4 mN/m; (2) PDMS, gl
= 18.7 mN/m; (3) octane, gl = 21.8 mN/m; (4) decane, gl = 23.9 mN/m;
(5) dodecane, gl = 25.4 mN/m; (6) hexadecane, gl = 27.6 mN/m; the critical
surface tensions are 5.7 mN/m (glass, squares), 6.6 mN/m (airplane wing, cir-
cles), 8.6 mN/m (aluminum, diamonds), 13.1 mN/m (car sheet, triangles).
Advancing contact angles were used for cos y.
Table 2. Zisman critical surface energies gc and the dispersive surface
energies gd
s according to the Girifalco±Good±Fowkes±Young equation of
thin complex films on different substrates.

For many solid-state polyelectrolyte±surfactant com-
plexes reported in the literature a supramolecular ordering
was observed, resembling that of liquid crystals.[24,25] Supra-
molecular and atomic ordering were investigated using
small- and wide-angle X-ray scattering experiments. X-ray
diffraction revealed the formation of well-ordered layer-
structures for PEI-Fluowet SB complexes prepared in two
different ways. The first complex was prepared as a pow-
der[12,13] and the second complex was prepared as a nano-
dispersion in water (Fig. 3). In the small-angle region, two
reflections are found, corresponding to a first- and third-
order reflection of a lamellar, smectic-A-like mesophase.
The reflex positions determine the long period to be d =
3.6 nm for the powder complex and d = 3.8 nm for the
nano-dispersion complex. The absence of second-order re-
flections indicates a symmetric lamellar two-phase system
that consists of two types of alternating layers. One layer is
strongly enriched with perfluorinated alkyl chains while
the second layer is enriched with polyelectrolyte chains and
ionic head groups. The thickness of both fluorinated and
ionic layers is about 1.8 nm and 1.9 nm for the powder
complex and for the nano-dispersion complex, respectively.
The slightly larger long period found for the nano-disper-
sion complex particles compared to that of the powder
complex can be explained by a less densely packed struc-
ture of the latter resulting from boundary effects of small
particles (curvature). In the wide-angle region only an
amorphous halo at about 2 nm±1 is found (Fig. 3), proving
the absence of any crystallinity. From this value an average
distance of about 0.5 nm results for the fluorinated alkyl
chains. In the curve of the powder complex (Fig. 3, curve a)
the halo is smaller than that found for the dispersion com-
plex (Fig. 3, curve b). This indicates a better packing of
fluorinated chains in the bulk material than in the disper-
sion, which again may be due to boundary effects of the
small particles. To estimate the stability of the dispersed
particles, the dispersion complex was freeze-dried. It was
found that the freeze-dried complex could be redispersed
with water. After this procedure the particle sizes increased
to 500 nm in diameter, which is significantly larger than
that of the pristine dispersion (200 nm). From this result,
we estimate that, on average, about 6 particles agglomerate
during drying and redispersion. The ability of the particles
to aggregate and fuse is essential for formation of homoge-
In conclusion, it was shown that waterborne nano-disper-
sions with a supramolecular ordering could be prepared
using a commercially available polyelectrolyte and a fluori-
nated surfactant. From this complex, thin coatings on
chemically different surfaces can be prepared at room tem-
perature without using organic solvents. The resulting coat-
ings are smooth, optically invisible, and have low surface
energies. Critical surface tensions, according to Zisman,[16]
were determined to be in the range from 5.7 to 13.1 mN/m
and dispersive surface energies according to the Girifalco±
Good±Fowkes±Young equation[17,18] are in the range from
The low surface energies observed for fluorinated poly-
electrolyte surfactant complexes as well as their liquid-crys-
talline-like structures are caused by the strong segregation
tendency of fluorinated and ionic moieties.
Fluowet SB (Clariant) is a surfactant solution containing 28 % sodium
(sulfonsuccinic acid ester) esterified with fluorinated alkyl chains. The pre-
dominant (95 %) length of the perfluorinated chains is six. The size of the
complex particles was measured by photon correlation spectroscopy using a
Zetamaster S (Malvern, UK). The z-average was about 200 nm (polydisper-
sity 0.20) 24 hours after preparation, and about 500 nm (polydispersity 0.4)
after freeze-drying and redispersion. The coatings were prepared by

0935-9648/99/0403-0323 $ 17.50+.50/0
Fig. 3. X-ray scattering curves of poly(ethyleneimine)-Fluowet SB com-
plexes as a function of the scattering vector (s = 2/l sin y; y is the angle be-
tween incident and scattered radiation). The scattering patterns indicate
lamellar mesomorphic smectic-A-like structures: a) complex powder made
by the classical precipitation procedure described earlier [13] and b) com-
plex dispersion with particle sizes of about 200 nm. The fitted curves consist
of Lorentzians for determination of structure parameters. For the powder
(a) the long period was determined to be d = 3.6 nm and for the dispersion
d = 3.8 nm. The broad reflection in both curves at about 2 nm±1 represents
an amorphous halo of the fluorinated alkyl chains.
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/0403-0324 $ 17.50+.50/0
distribution of 2.0 ml of a 0.32 % (w/w) complex-dispersion uniformly on a
100 ´ 100 mm large sheet at 50 C. For this an air-brush was employed. The
complex was dried for 30 min at 70 C in an air stream, followed by washing
with water for 5 min and subsequent drying for 30 min at 70 C. Washing
with water was performed to remove excess surfactant and lithium ions. In
the final step the complex surface was polished with a soft cotton tissue. This
procedure results in coatings with a thickness of about 400 nm as deter-
mined using a Surface profiler Dektak3ST from Veeco Instruments. Contact
angle measurements were performed on a Krüss G10 contact angle goni-
ometer. The contact angles reported are the average from five measure-
ments. The advancing contact angle was read by injecting a 5 mL liquid drop.
The receding contact angle was measured by removing about 3 mL of liquid
from the drop, and the static contact angle was obtained from a drop (ca.
5 mL) on the surface. Linear alkanes, methyl-terminated poly-(dimethylsi-
loxane) oligomer (Geltest Inc.), a-bromonaphthalene, diiodomethane and
water (millipore) were used as standards to measure the critical surface ten-
sion. Wide angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) measurements were carried out
with a Nonius PDS120 powder diffractometer in transmission geometry. A
FR590 generator was used as the source for Cu Ka radiation, monochroma-
tization of the primary beam was achieved by means of a curved Ge crystal,
and the scattered radiation was measured with a Nonius CPS120 position-
sensitive detector. The resolution of this detector is better than 0.018.
Small-angle scattering measurements were recorded with an X-ray vacu-
um camera with a pinhole collimator (Anton Paar, Austria; model A-8054)
equipped with image plates (type BAS III, Fuji, Japan). The image plates
were read with a MACScience Dip-Scanner IPR-420 and IP reader DIPR-

Morphosynthesis of Calcium Carbonate
By Dominic Walsh, Benedicte Lebeau, and Stephen Mann*
The chemical construction and patterning of inorganic
materials with unusual and complex form (ªmorphosynthe-
sisº)[1] is an important development in the science of orga-
nized matter.[2] A number of imaginative routes to prepar-
ing such materials have been recently reported. These are
based on several underlying principles: i) use of endogen-
ous templates such as self-assembled supramolecular struc-
tures,[3,4] lyotropic liquid crystals,[5,6] block copolymer
mesophases,[7,8] colloidal arrays,[9,10] and bacterial super-
structures;[11] ii) induction of morphological complexity via
topological defect structures, torsional instability, and inter-
biphases;[12±15] and iii) inorganic replication of organized
reaction fields such as reverse micelles,[16,17] bicontinuous
microemulsions,[18,19] oil-in-water droplets,[20] and emulsion
foams.[21±23] Together, these approaches offer the possibility
of synthesizing a wide range of complex inorganic materials
for numerous applications. For example, hollow inorganic
shells with controlled meso- and macro-porosity might find
uses in catalysis, separations technology, and biomedical
engineering. Schacht et al.[20] produced hollow shells of me-
sostructured silica by templating the reaction at the surface
of oil droplets dispersed in acidified aqueous solution.
Likewise, we have previously prepared hollow shells of
crystalline calcium carbonate (aragonite) with controlled
meso-porosity,[23] although the procedure gave low yields
and involved several stages, including the use of extraneous
polymer-bead templates. Clearly, such materials will only
be technologically relevant if inexpensive routes with high
product yields can be developed. In light of this important
consideration, we now report a facile one-step, high-yield
approach to the preparation of discrete spheroidal micro-
meter-sized particles of crystalline calcium carbonate (va-
terite) with an elaborate sponge-like microarchitecture. We
show that the passive evaporation of water-in-oil micro-
emulsions containing supersaturated droplets of calcium bi-
carbonate solution produces patterned mineral particles
commensurate in size with the diameter of the water drop-
lets. The spheroids are formed by nucleation of vaterite at
the anionic head groups of sodium dodecylsulfate mole-
